title: SEGMENT 14 — Viromics: the bacteriophage component and the dark matter

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Viromics: the bacteriophage component and the dark matter
Everything we've talked about so far has been about cellular microbes — bacteria, archaea, eukaryotes.
But the most numerous biological entities in your gut, in the ocean, in soil, are not cells at all.

They are viruses, almost all of which are bacteriophages — viruses that infect bacteria — and they constitute what is sometimes called the dark matter of the microbiome, because we know they're there in huge numbers but we know remarkably little about what they're doing.
The numbers are arresting.
In a typical environment with abundant bacterial life, virus particles outnumber bacterial cells by roughly ten to one.

There are estimated to be around 10^31 virus particles on Earth, which is more than every other kind of biological entity combined and probably greater than the number of stars in the visible universe.
In your gut, there are an estimated 10^15 virus particles, mostly bacteriophages, doing the work of constantly culling and reshaping the bacterial community.
Phages are the dominant predators of bacteria; they are agents of horizontal gene transfer, shuttling genes between hosts; they sculpt bacterial population dynamics on hourly to daily timescales.
The subfield that studies viral communities by sequencing is called viromics, and methodologically it has been the hardest of the microbiome subfields to develop.
The reason is that viruses don't have a universal marker gene like the 16S rRNA.
Bacteria all have 16S; archaea all have 16S; you can use one set of PCR primers to amplify and identify every bacterium and archaeon on Earth.
There is no equivalent for viruses.
Different viral families have different genes, different genome types (DNA versus RNA, single-stranded versus double-stranded), different gene contents, and no single gene is present in all of them.
So you can't do a 16S-equivalent survey for viruses.
You have to use shotgun metagenomics, ideally with a viral enrichment step that physically separates virus-sized particles from cellular ones before extracting DNA.

The viral enrichment workflow typically involves filtering the sample to remove cells (using filters with pore sizes of 0.22 or 0.45 microns, which lets virus particles through but excludes most bacteria), then concentrating the virus-sized particles by ultracentrifugation or tangential flow filtration, then treating with DNase to destroy free DNA, then lysing the virus particles and extracting their nucleic acids.
Even with this enrichment, viral metagenomes are noisy and contain substantial bacterial contamination.
And once you have the sequences, you face the second hard problem: most viral sequences don't match anything in any database.
Bacterial and archaeal reference genomes are now numerous and well-annotated; viral reference genomes are sparse and biased toward viruses of human or agricultural importance.
The vast majority of phages in any environmental sample produce sequences that are unrecognizable, which is exactly why this is the "dark matter.
Despite these difficulties, the field has produced some major insights.
The crAssphage discovery in 2014 by Robert Edwards's group at San Diego State is a great example.

By doing cross-assembly of human gut metagenomes — assembling sequences that appeared across many independent samples — they identified an extremely abundant phage that had been hiding in plain sight in every gut metagenome ever produced.

CrAssphage turned out to be the most abundant phage in human guts, present in something like half of all people sampled, and entirely missed by previous viral databases because nobody had ever cultured its host.
The discovery prompted the recognition of a whole new family of phages, the Crassvirales, and the realization that the gut virome is dominated by uncharacterized phage groups.
The Gut Virome Database and other recent efforts have now produced catalogs of hundreds of thousands of distinct phage genomes assembled from gut metagenomes worldwide.

The dominant phages are temperate phages of common gut bacteria — Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes, Bifidobacterium — that integrate into bacterial genomes as prophages and reactivate periodically to produce new virions.
Lytic phages, the kind that just infect and lyse cells without integrating, are present too but less abundant in the steady-state gut.
The functional impact of the gut virome is starting to be characterized.
Phages can carry auxiliary metabolic genes — bacterial-origin genes that the phage has acquired and now expresses during infection, often modifying host metabolism.
Phages are also major drivers of bacterial pangenome diversity, because they shuttle genes between bacterial hosts during lysogenic conversion.

Some studies have associated specific viral community shifts with IBD, with C. diff infection (where altered virome composition contributes to dysbiosis and recovery), and with response to immunotherapy.

There's also intense interest in phage therapy — using carefully selected phages to treat bacterial infections, particularly antibiotic-resistant ones — which has had some dramatic clinical successes in compassionate-use cases and is being developed commercially by several groups.
Here is the analogy.
The viral component of the microbiome is like the predator layer of an ecosystem you only just realized existed.
You've spent decades cataloging the herbivores — the bacterial cells — and you've ignored the predators that have been culling, shaping, and rearranging the herbivore populations the whole time.

Now you've started looking at the predators, and you've discovered there are ten times more of them than you thought, that most of them are unknown species, and that they may matter enormously for the dynamics of the community you'd been studying.
The herbivore-only picture was incomplete in a fundamental way.
The field is moving fast, but it's still less mature than bacterial microbiomics.

Reference databases are growing, computational tools for identifying viral sequences in metagenomes (like VirSorter, DeepVirFinder, VIBRANT) are improving, and the integration of viromics into routine microbiome studies is becoming more common.
But we are essentially in the 1990s of viromics compared to where we are in bacterial microbiomics.
There's a lot still to find.
